segments.text: Apoptosis:Programmed Cell Death

Necrosis
Cell death due to acute injury
Cells typically swell and burst
Inflammation results
Morphology of necrotic cell

Apoptosis
Cell shrinks and condenses
Cytoskeleton collapses
Nuclear envelope disassembles
Nuclear DNA fragments
Dying cell alerts other cells for phagocytosis
Cell morphology during apoptosis
Phagocytosis of apoptotic cell
Programmed cell death is a normal part of development

The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2002
"for their discoveries concerning 'genetic regulation of organ development and programmed cell death'"
Caenorhabditis elegans

C. elegans life cycle
Dauer (German for long-lasting larva) can live at least 6 months in a dormant state

Hermaphrodite has 959 somatic cells, 131 die 	during development
Male has 1031 cells, 147 die during development
C. elegans develops in a specific lineage pathway
PCD in normal C. elegans development

Important terms/concepts regarding
C. elegans
C. elegans as a model system
clear body
cell lineage development
relatively small invariant number of somatic cells
Hermaphrodite and male

ced
cell-death abnormal

ced-1 mutant
Mutation blocks  engulfment of dead cells, so they accumulate. “Persistent corpse” phenotype
Mutaganize ced-1 mutants
ced-1/ced-3 mutants
No refractile dead cells, because cell death has not occurred. Easy to spot.
Used this screen to find mutations in PCD
C. elegans PCD mutants
Dead cells
Initially, over 100 mutations in 14 genes were isolated that were involved at various steps of Apoptosis

ced-3 and ced-4
PCD does not occur
all 1090 cells survive
(both are “loss of function mutations”)

ced-9
In original mutant, there was no cell death.  Analysis of this mutant, it was a “gain of function mutation”
-In loss of function mutants, all 1090 cells die

ced-9 loss of function/ced-3 double mutants
no cell death

Summary of major CED proteins in C. elegans
Normally CED-9 is complexed with CED-4 and CED-3 keeping CED-3 inactive
Apoptosis stimuli causes CED-9 dissociation
CED-4 binds to CED-3 and promotes CED-3 activation.
CED-3 is activated by cleaving itself
cell is committed to die

Caspases
Cysteine aspartate specific proteases
Key proteolytic enzymes that carry out PCD
Cysteine containing proteases (cysteine at 	active site)
Cleave just C terminal to Aspartate
Have 3 domains
Range from 30 to 50 kd
ced-3 is a Caspase

Zymogen
The inactive precursor form of an enyzme.  Zymogens are typically activated by proteolytic cleavage

Inactive
Zymogen
Proteolytic
cut
Caspase Activation
Caspase activation and effects

Proteolysis cascade
Initiating caspase
Caspase Y
Caspase Z
Caspases cleave nuclear lamins

Caspase substrates
Other caspases
Nuclear lamins
Inhibitors of deoxyribonucleases
Activates actin filament cleaving enzymes (gelsolin)
Negative regulators of apoptosis (bcl-2 proteins)
Over 40 identified, but not completely understood

Regulation by proteolysis
Proteolysis is irreversible
Most proteases are synthesized as inactive precursors
Proteases can regulate their own activation
Where there are proteases there are inhibitors
Proteolytic reactions can be specific
Mammalian caspase homology and structure

C. elegans PCD molecules and pathways are conserved with humans
Bcl-2 is B Cell Lymphoma

Cell murder by cytotoxic T cells
DD-Death Domain
DED-Death Effector Domain
In humans, there are multiple interacting pathways
Mitochondria is intimately involved in apoptosis

Normal human allele of the bcl-2 gene can supress C. elegans ced-9 loss of function mutants
Normal bcl-2, like normal ced-9 is a cell death inhibitor
Mutant (overexpressing) bcl-2 helps to cause tumors by blocking apoptosis in humans.
When cells that are told to commit suicide and don’t, they can contribute to tumors.
Specific example of C. elegans PCD relationship to humans, and its involvement with human cancers
Many of the other ced mutants were found to be defective in cleaning up the corpses

Apoptosis/Programmed cell death
Necrosis
C. elegans (characteristics as a model organism)

Proteolysis vs phosphorylation
bcl-2/ced-9
